Evaluate stool pattern, discomfort, and response to interventions.

stem: The nurse prepares to administer a scheduled anticonvulsant. Drag the medication-administration actions into the correct order.
rationale: Safe medication administration requires review, label-to-MAR checks, client identification/parameters, administration, and monitoring/documentation.
steps: Review the medication order, allergies, indication, relevant labs, and contraindications.
Compare the medication label with the medication administration record during preparation.
Identify the client using two approved identifiers and assess required parameters.
Administer the medication by the correct route and at the correct time.
Document administration and monitor for therapeutic and adverse effects.

stem: The nurse is preparing rapid-acting insulin before breakfast. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Insulin safety requires glucose and meal assessment, verification of type/dose, correct preparation, timing with food, and monitoring/documentation.
steps: Check the blood glucose and review the insulin prescription and meal status.
Verify the insulin type, dose, expiration, and appearance with facility-required checks.
Prepare the insulin using the correct syringe or pen technique.
Administer the insulin when the meal is available according to the insulin onset.
Monitor for hypoglycemia and document dose, site, and blood glucose.

stem: In the clinic, a client with diabetes is awake, able to swallow, and has symptoms of hypoglycemia. Drag the treatment actions into the correct order.
rationale: For a conscious client, verify glucose, give fast carbohydrate, recheck, repeat or provide longer-acting food as indicated, and document.
steps: Check a point-of-care blood glucose level.
Give a fast-acting carbohydrate if the glucose is low and the client can swallow safely.
Recheck the blood glucose after the expected treatment interval.
Repeat treatment or provide longer-acting carbohydrate/protein according to protocol and meal timing.
Document the event, treatment, response, and possible cause.

stem: A client receiving digoxin reports poor appetite. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: Digoxin concerns require assessment, review of relevant labs/renal function, holding for parameters/toxicity cues, provider notification, and documentation.
steps: Assess apical pulse, blood pressure, symptoms, and cardiac rhythm if available.
Review potassium level, renal function, and the digoxin prescription.
Hold digoxin if ordered parameters or toxicity cues are present.
Notify the provider of abnormal findings and request further direction.
Document assessment findings, held dose if applicable, and provider instructions.

stem: An older adult client receiving heparin has hematuria. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Heparin-related bleeding requires immediate assessment, holding/stopping according to protocol, provider notification, bleeding precautions, and documentation.
steps: Assess bleeding, vital signs, pain, neurologic status, and hemodynamic stability.
Stop or hold the infusion/next dose according to protocol if significant bleeding or critical lab results are present.
Notify the provider and prepare for prescribed laboratory testing or reversal therapy.
Institute bleeding precautions and monitor for worsening bleeding.
Document findings, actions, notifications, and client response.

stem: During a blood transfusion, An adult client develops fever and flushing. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: A suspected transfusion reaction requires stopping blood first, maintaining IV access with saline, assessing the client, notifying provider/blood bank, and returning product/tubing with documentation.
steps: Stop the transfusion immediately.
Keep the IV line open with normal saline using new tubing.
Assess vital signs, airway, breathing, circulation, and symptoms.
Notify the provider and blood bank according to policy.
Send the blood product and tubing to the blood bank and document the reaction.

stem: An older adult client receiving opioid analgesia has respiratory rate of 8/min. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Opioid oversedation requires airway/respiratory assessment, holding opioids, support and help, naloxone if indicated, and reassessment/documentation.
steps: Attempt to arouse the client and assess airway, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, sedation level, and pain.
Stop or hold additional opioid medication.
Call for help and support ventilation/oxygenation as needed.
Administer naloxone if prescribed or per emergency protocol.
Reassess respiratory status, pain, and recurrence of sedation and document.

stem: While maintenance IV fluid is infusing through a peripheral IV, the nurse notes swelling and coolness at the site. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Infiltration/extravasation requires stopping infusion, assessment, medication-specific management, catheter removal or protocol care, supportive measures, notification, and documentation.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately.
Assess the IV site, distal circulation, pain, and medication infusing.
Disconnect tubing and aspirate any residual medication if extravasation protocol indicates.
Remove the IV catheter or follow vesicant-specific protocol.
Elevate the extremity, apply ordered compresses if indicated, notify the provider/pharmacy, and document.

stem: A client is starting parenteral nutrition through a central line. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Parenteral nutrition requires verification, baseline assessment, correct pump/dedicated access, aseptic initiation, and close monitoring.
steps: Verify the prescription, solution label, rate, central-line placement status, and compatibility.
Obtain baseline glucose, weight, intake/output, and relevant laboratory values.
Use an infusion pump and dedicated lumen or line as required.
Start the infusion at the prescribed rate using aseptic technique.
Monitor glucose, line infection signs, fluid status, and laboratory trends.

stem: A client has a potassium level of 3.0 mEq/L and is prescribed IV potassium replacement. Drag the safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: IV potassium requires renal/urine/rhythm assessment, confirmation of dilution/no IV push, pump-controlled rate, monitoring, and follow-up labs/documentation.
steps: Review renal function, urine output, cardiac rhythm, and the potassium prescription.
Verify that potassium is diluted in IV fluid and never prepared for IV push.
Use an infusion pump and administer at the prescribed safe rate.
Monitor the IV site and cardiac rhythm or symptoms according to acuity and policy.
Recheck potassium level as prescribed and document response.

stem: A client receiving care needs teaching about elevated INR education. Drag the teaching actions into the correct order.
rationale: Warfarin teaching begins with assessment, dose/INR teaching, bleeding precautions, interaction review, and teach-back/documentation.
steps: Assess understanding of anticoagulation purpose, bleeding history, diet, medications, and follow-up access.
Teach prescribed dose schedule, INR monitoring, and what to do for missed doses according to instructions.
Teach bleeding precautions and when to seek urgent care.
Review medication and food interactions and the need to consult before new medications or supplements.
Use teach-back and document understanding and follow-up plan.

stem: A client is receiving amikacin. Drag the medication-monitoring actions into the correct order.
rationale: Aminoglycosides require renal/ototoxicity assessment, level schedule verification, correct administration, toxicity monitoring, and reporting/documentation.
steps: Review culture data, allergies, renal function, hearing/balance history, and other nephrotoxic medications.
Verify dose, timing, infusion rate, and ordered peak/trough or drug-level schedule.
Administer the medication as prescribed using the correct IV compatibility and rate.
Monitor urine output, creatinine, hearing changes, tinnitus, and balance problems.
Report abnormal drug levels or toxicity cues and document response.

stem: During a antineoplastic infusion, the client reports burning at the IV site. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Suspected vesicant extravasation requires stopping infusion and leaving the catheter, assessing, activating protocol, aspirating/administering antidote if prescribed, and documentation/follow-up.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately while leaving the catheter in place.
Assess the site, pain, swelling, blood return, and medication involved.
Notify the provider/pharmacy and follow the vesicant extravasation protocol.
Aspirate residual medication and administer antidote if prescribed by protocol.
Document the event, site appearance, interventions, teaching, and follow-up monitoring.

stem: A newly admitted client has client asks how to use the PCA pump related to patient-controlled analgesia. Drag the PCA safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: PCA safety requires assessment, teaching that only the client presses the button, pump verification, ongoing monitoring, and documentation.
steps: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory status, understanding, and who has access to the button.
Teach that only the client should press the PCA button.
Verify pump settings, medication, lockout interval, and safety alarms with another nurse as required.
Monitor pain relief, sedation, respiratory rate, oxygenation, and adverse effects.
Document teaching, assessments, dose totals, and client response.

stem: An older adult client has a central line issue: loose dressing. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.

steps: Assess the insertion site, dressing integrity, line patency, symptoms, and infection cues.
Perform hand hygiene and use aseptic technique for any line access or dressing care.
Scrub the hub or needleless connector according to policy before access.
Administer compatible medication through the correct lumen at the prescribed rate.
Monitor for infiltration, infection, occlusion, and therapeutic or adverse effects.

Compare the clarified home list with admission or transfer orders.
Identify omissions, duplications, interactions, and dose differences.
Notify the provider or pharmacist for reconciliation of unresolved discrepancies.
Document the updated medication list and client education.

Prepare the insulin using the correct syringe or pen technique.
Administer the insulin when the meal is available according to the insulin onset.
Monitor for hypoglycemia and document dose, site, and blood glucose.

stem: In the school nurse office, a client with diabetes is awake, able to swallow, and has symptoms of hypoglycemia. Drag the treatment actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the outpatient clinic; client age 27.
rationale: For a conscious client, verify glucose, give fast carbohydrate, recheck, repeat or provide longer-acting food as indicated, and document.
steps: Check a point-of-care blood glucose level.
Give a fast-acting carbohydrate if the glucose is low and the client can swallow safely.
Recheck the blood glucose after the expected treatment interval.
Repeat treatment or provide longer-acting carbohydrate/protein according to protocol and meal timing.
Document the event, treatment, response, and possible cause.

stem: An adult client receiving digoxin reports blurred yellow vision. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: Digoxin concerns require assessment, review of relevant labs/renal function, holding for parameters/toxicity cues, provider notification, and documentation.
steps: Assess apical pulse, blood pressure, symptoms, and cardiac rhythm if available.
Review potassium level, renal function, and the digoxin prescription.
Hold digoxin if ordered parameters or toxicity cues are present.
Notify the provider of abnormal findings and request further direction.
Document assessment findings, held dose if applicable, and provider instructions.

stem: A newly admitted client receiving heparin has gum bleeding. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Heparin-related bleeding requires immediate assessment, holding/stopping according to protocol, provider notification, bleeding precautions, and documentation.
steps: Assess bleeding, vital signs, pain, neurologic status, and hemodynamic stability.
Stop or hold the infusion/next dose according to protocol if significant bleeding or critical lab results are present.
Notify the provider and prepare for prescribed laboratory testing or reversal therapy.
Institute bleeding precautions and monitor for worsening bleeding.
Document findings, actions, notifications, and client response.

stem: During a blood transfusion, An older adult client develops dyspnea and hives. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: A suspected transfusion reaction requires stopping blood first, maintaining IV access with saline, assessing the client, notifying provider/blood bank, and returning product/tubing with documentation.
steps: Stop the transfusion immediately.
Keep the IV line open with normal saline using new tubing.
Assess vital signs, airway, breathing, circulation, and symptoms.
Notify the provider and blood bank according to policy.
Send the blood product and tubing to the blood bank and document the reaction.

stem: An adult client receiving opioid analgesia has oxygen saturation dropping. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Opioid oversedation requires airway/respiratory assessment, holding opioids, support and help, naloxone if indicated, and reassessment/documentation.
steps: Attempt to arouse the client and assess airway, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, sedation level, and pain.
Stop or hold additional opioid medication.
Call for help and support ventilation/oxygenation as needed.
Administer naloxone if prescribed or per emergency protocol.
Reassess respiratory status, pain, and recurrence of sedation and document.

stem: While contrast infusion is infusing through a peripheral IV, the nurse notes swelling and coolness at the site. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the step-down unit; client age 19.
rationale: Infiltration/extravasation requires stopping infusion, assessment, medication-specific management, catheter removal or protocol care, supportive measures, notification, and documentation.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately.
Assess the IV site, distal circulation, pain, and medication infusing.
Disconnect tubing and aspirate any residual medication if extravasation protocol indicates.
Remove the IV catheter or follow vesicant-specific protocol.
Elevate the extremity, apply ordered compresses if indicated, notify the provider/pharmacy, and document.

stem: A client receiving care is starting parenteral nutrition through a central line. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the community clinic; client age 34.
rationale: Parenteral nutrition requires verification, baseline assessment, correct pump/dedicated access, aseptic initiation, and close monitoring.
steps: Verify the prescription, solution label, rate, central-line placement status, and compatibility.
Obtain baseline glucose, weight, intake/output, and relevant laboratory values.
Use an infusion pump and dedicated lumen or line as required.
Start the infusion at the prescribed rate using aseptic technique.
Monitor glucose, line infection signs, fluid status, and laboratory trends.

stem: A client receiving care has a potassium level of 3.2 mEq/L and is prescribed IV potassium replacement. Drag the safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: IV potassium requires renal/urine/rhythm assessment, confirmation of dilution/no IV push, pump-controlled rate, monitoring, and follow-up labs/documentation.
steps: Review renal function, urine output, cardiac rhythm, and the potassium prescription.
Verify that potassium is diluted in IV fluid and never prepared for IV push.
Use an infusion pump and administer at the prescribed safe rate.
Monitor the IV site and cardiac rhythm or symptoms according to acuity and policy.
Recheck potassium level as prescribed and document response.

stem: A newly admitted client needs teaching about new warfarin prescription. Drag the teaching actions into the correct order.
rationale: Warfarin teaching begins with assessment, dose/INR teaching, bleeding precautions, interaction review, and teach-back/documentation.
steps: Assess understanding of anticoagulation purpose, bleeding history, diet, medications, and follow-up access.
Teach prescribed dose schedule, INR monitoring, and what to do for missed doses according to instructions.
Teach bleeding precautions and when to seek urgent care.
Review medication and food interactions and the need to consult before new medications or supplements.
Use teach-back and document understanding and follow-up plan.

stem: A newly admitted client is receiving gentamicin. Drag the medication-monitoring actions into the correct order.
rationale: Aminoglycosides require renal/ototoxicity assessment, level schedule verification, correct administration, toxicity monitoring, and reporting/documentation.
steps: Review culture data, allergies, renal function, hearing/balance history, and other nephrotoxic medications.
Verify dose, timing, infusion rate, and ordered peak/trough or drug-level schedule.
Administer the medication as prescribed using the correct IV compatibility and rate.
Monitor urine output, creatinine, hearing changes, tinnitus, and balance problems.
Report abnormal drug levels or toxicity cues and document response.

stem: During a vesicant chemotherapy, the client reports burning at the IV site. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: evening shift in the rehabilitation unit; client age 62.
rationale: Suspected vesicant extravasation requires stopping infusion and leaving the catheter, assessing, activating protocol, aspirating/administering antidote if prescribed, and documentation/follow-up.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately while leaving the catheter in place.
Assess the site, pain, swelling, blood return, and medication involved.
Notify the provider/pharmacy and follow the vesicant extravasation protocol.
Aspirate residual medication and administer antidote if prescribed by protocol.
Document the event, site appearance, interventions, teaching, and follow-up monitoring.

stem: A newly admitted client has family member pressing the button related to patient-controlled analgesia. Drag the PCA safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: PCA safety requires assessment, teaching that only the client presses the button, pump verification, ongoing monitoring, and documentation.
steps: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory status, understanding, and who has access to the button.
Teach that only the client should press the PCA button.
Verify pump settings, medication, lockout interval, and safety alarms with another nurse as required.
Monitor pain relief, sedation, respiratory rate, oxygenation, and adverse effects.
Document teaching, assessments, dose totals, and client response.

stem: A newly admitted client has a central line issue: new redness at insertion site. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Central-line medication care requires assessment, aseptic technique, hub disinfection, correct lumen/compatibility/rate, and ongoing monitoring.
steps: Assess the insertion site, dressing integrity, line patency, symptoms, and infection cues.
Perform hand hygiene and use aseptic technique for any line access or dressing care.
Scrub the hub or needleless connector according to policy before access.
Administer compatible medication through the correct lumen at the prescribed rate.
Monitor for infiltration, infection, occlusion, and therapeutic or adverse effects.

stem: During medication reconciliation, the nurse finds this discrepancy: two different doses of levothyroxine listed. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the home health visit; client age 43.
rationale: Medication reconciliation requires clarifying the home list, comparing orders, identifying discrepancies, resolving with provider/pharmacist, and documenting.
steps: Clarify the medication history with the client, caregiver, pharmacy record, or prior medication list.
Compare the clarified home list with admission or transfer orders.
Identify omissions, duplications, interactions, and dose differences.
Notify the provider or pharmacist for reconciliation of unresolved discrepancies.
Document the updated medication list and client education.

stem: The nurse prepares to administer a scheduled anticonvulsant. Drag the medication-administration actions into the correct order. Additional context: evening shift in the community clinic; client age 68.
rationale: Safe medication administration requires review, label-to-MAR checks, client identification/parameters, administration, and monitoring/documentation.
steps: Review the medication order, allergies, indication, relevant labs, and contraindications.
Compare the medication label with the medication administration record during preparation.
Identify the client using two approved identifiers and assess required parameters.
Administer the medication by the correct route and at the correct time.
Document administration and monitor for therapeutic and adverse effects.

Administer naloxone if prescribed or per emergency protocol.
Reassess respiratory status, pain, and recurrence of sedation and document.

stem: While potassium-containing fluid is infusing through a peripheral IV, the nurse notes swelling and coolness at the site. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the long-term care unit; client age 19.
rationale: Infiltration/extravasation requires stopping infusion, assessment, medication-specific management, catheter removal or protocol care, supportive measures, notification, and documentation.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately.
Assess the IV site, distal circulation, pain, and medication infusing.
Disconnect tubing and aspirate any residual medication if extravasation protocol indicates.
Remove the IV catheter or follow vesicant-specific protocol.
Elevate the extremity, apply ordered compresses if indicated, notify the provider/pharmacy, and document.

stem: A client receiving care is starting parenteral nutrition through a central line. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the rehabilitation unit; client age 19.
rationale: Parenteral nutrition requires verification, baseline assessment, correct pump/dedicated access, aseptic initiation, and close monitoring.
steps: Verify the prescription, solution label, rate, central-line placement status, and compatibility.
Obtain baseline glucose, weight, intake/output, and relevant laboratory values.
Use an infusion pump and dedicated lumen or line as required.
Start the infusion at the prescribed rate using aseptic technique.
Monitor glucose, line infection signs, fluid status, and laboratory trends.

stem: An older adult client has a potassium level of 2.8 mEq/L and is prescribed IV potassium replacement. Drag the safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: IV potassium requires renal/urine/rhythm assessment, confirmation of dilution/no IV push, pump-controlled rate, monitoring, and follow-up labs/documentation.
steps: Review renal function, urine output, cardiac rhythm, and the potassium prescription.
Verify that potassium is diluted in IV fluid and never prepared for IV push.
Use an infusion pump and administer at the prescribed safe rate.
Monitor the IV site and cardiac rhythm or symptoms according to acuity and policy.
Recheck potassium level as prescribed and document response.

stem: An adult client needs teaching about elevated INR education. Drag the teaching actions into the correct order.
rationale: Warfarin teaching begins with assessment, dose/INR teaching, bleeding precautions, interaction review, and teach-back/documentation.
steps: Assess understanding of anticoagulation purpose, bleeding history, diet, medications, and follow-up access.
Teach prescribed dose schedule, INR monitoring, and what to do for missed doses according to instructions.
Teach bleeding precautions and when to seek urgent care.
Review medication and food interactions and the need to consult before new medications or supplements.
Use teach-back and document understanding and follow-up plan.

stem: A client receiving care is receiving gentamicin. Drag the medication-monitoring actions into the correct order.
rationale: Aminoglycosides require renal/ototoxicity assessment, level schedule verification, correct administration, toxicity monitoring, and reporting/documentation.
steps: Review culture data, allergies, renal function, hearing/balance history, and other nephrotoxic medications.
Verify dose, timing, infusion rate, and ordered peak/trough or drug-level schedule.
Administer the medication as prescribed using the correct IV compatibility and rate.
Monitor urine output, creatinine, hearing changes, tinnitus, and balance problems.
Report abnormal drug levels or toxicity cues and document response.

stem: During a peripheral chemotherapy infusion, the client reports burning at the IV site. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: evening shift in the rehabilitation unit; client age 34.
rationale: Suspected vesicant extravasation requires stopping infusion and leaving the catheter, assessing, activating protocol, aspirating/administering antidote if prescribed, and documentation/follow-up.
steps: Stop the infusion immediately while leaving the catheter in place.
Assess the site, pain, swelling, blood return, and medication involved.
Notify the provider/pharmacy and follow the vesicant extravasation protocol.
Aspirate residual medication and administer antidote if prescribed by protocol.
Document the event, site appearance, interventions, teaching, and follow-up monitoring.

stem: A client has new PCA setup related to patient-controlled analgesia. Drag the PCA safety actions into the correct order.
rationale: PCA safety requires assessment, teaching that only the client presses the button, pump verification, ongoing monitoring, and documentation.
steps: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory status, understanding, and who has access to the button.
Teach that only the client should press the PCA button.
Verify pump settings, medication, lockout interval, and safety alarms with another nurse as required.
Monitor pain relief, sedation, respiratory rate, oxygenation, and adverse effects.
Document teaching, assessments, dose totals, and client response.

stem: A client receiving care has a central line issue: damp dressing. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Central-line medication care requires assessment, aseptic technique, hub disinfection, correct lumen/compatibility/rate, and ongoing monitoring.
steps: Assess the insertion site, dressing integrity, line patency, symptoms, and infection cues.
Perform hand hygiene and use aseptic technique for any line access or dressing care.
Scrub the hub or needleless connector according to policy before access.
Administer compatible medication through the correct lumen at the prescribed rate.
Monitor for infiltration, infection, occlusion, and therapeutic or adverse effects.

stem: During medication reconciliation, the nurse finds this discrepancy: home beta blocker missing from the admission list. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the postoperative unit; client age 27.
rationale: Medication reconciliation requires clarifying the home list, comparing orders, identifying discrepancies, resolving with provider/pharmacist, and documenting.
steps: Clarify the medication history with the client, caregiver, pharmacy record, or prior medication list.
Compare the clarified home list with admission or transfer orders.
Identify omissions, duplications, interactions, and dose differences.
Notify the provider or pharmacist for reconciliation of unresolved discrepancies.
Document the updated medication list and client education.

stem: The nurse prepares to administer a oral antihypertensive. Drag the medication-administration actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the outpatient clinic; client age 74.
rationale: Safe medication administration requires review, label-to-MAR checks, client identification/parameters, administration, and monitoring/documentation.
steps: Review the medication order, allergies, indication, relevant labs, and contraindications.
Compare the medication label with the medication administration record during preparation.
Identify the client using two approved identifiers and assess required parameters.
Administer the medication by the correct route and at the correct time.
Document administration and monitor for therapeutic and adverse effects.

stem: The nurse is preparing rapid-acting insulin before breakfast. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the community clinic; client age 34.
rationale: Insulin safety requires glucose and meal assessment, verification of type/dose, correct preparation, timing with food, and monitoring/documentation.
steps: Check the blood glucose and review the insulin prescription and meal status.
Verify the insulin type, dose, expiration, and appearance with facility-required checks.
Prepare the insulin using the correct syringe or pen technique.
Administer the insulin when the meal is available according to the insulin onset.
Monitor for hypoglycemia and document dose, site, and blood glucose.

stem: In the school nurse office, a client with diabetes is awake, able to swallow, and has symptoms of hypoglycemia. Drag the treatment actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the emergency department; client age 82.
rationale: For a conscious client, verify glucose, give fast carbohydrate, recheck, repeat or provide longer-acting food as indicated, and document.
steps: Check a point-of-care blood glucose level.
Give a fast-acting carbohydrate if the glucose is low and the client can swallow safely.
Recheck the blood glucose after the expected treatment interval.
Repeat treatment or provide longer-acting carbohydrate/protein according to protocol and meal timing.
Document the event, treatment, response, and possible cause.

stem: A client receiving care receiving digoxin reports blurred yellow vision. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: Digoxin concerns require assessment, review of relevant labs/renal function, holding for parameters/toxicity cues, provider notification, and documentation.
steps: Assess apical pulse, blood pressure, symptoms, and cardiac rhythm if available.
Review potassium level, renal function, and the digoxin prescription.
Hold digoxin if ordered parameters or toxicity cues are present.
Notify the provider of abnormal findings and request further direction.
Document assessment findings, held dose if applicable, and provider instructions.

stem: A client receiving care receiving heparin has black tarry stool. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Heparin-related bleeding requires immediate assessment, holding/stopping according to protocol, provider notification, bleeding precautions, and documentation.
steps: Assess bleeding, vital signs, pain, neurologic status, and hemodynamic stability.
Stop or hold the infusion/next dose according to protocol if significant bleeding or critical lab results are present.
Notify the provider and prepare for prescribed laboratory testing or reversal therapy.
Institute bleeding precautions and monitor for worsening bleeding.
Document findings, actions, notifications, and client response.

stem: During a blood transfusion, An older adult client develops wheezing during the first 15 minutes. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: A suspected transfusion reaction requires stopping blood first, maintaining IV access with saline, assessing the client, notifying provider/blood bank, and returning product/tubing with documentation.
steps: Stop the transfusion immediately.
Keep the IV line open with normal saline using new tubing.
Assess vital signs, airway, breathing, circulation, and symptoms.
Notify the provider and blood bank according to policy.
Send the blood product and tubing to the blood bank and document the reaction.

stem: A newly admitted client receiving opioid analgesia has oxygen saturation dropping. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Opioid oversedation requires airway/respiratory assessment, holding opioids, support and help, naloxone if indicated, and reassessment/documentation.
steps: Attempt to arouse the client and assess airway, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, sedation level, and pain.

Call for help and support ventilation/oxygenation as needed.
Administer naloxone if prescribed or per emergency protocol.
Reassess respiratory status, pain, and recurrence of sedation and document.

stem: During medication reconciliation, the nurse finds this discrepancy: client reports taking an herbal supplement. Drag the actions into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the outpatient clinic; client age 51.
rationale: Medication reconciliation requires clarifying the home list, comparing orders, identifying discrepancies, resolving with provider/pharmacist, and documenting.
steps: Clarify the medication history with the client, caregiver, pharmacy record, or prior medication list.
Compare the clarified home list with admission or transfer orders.
Identify omissions, duplications, interactions, and dose differences.
Notify the provider or pharmacist for reconciliation of unresolved discrepancies.
Document the updated medication list and client education.

stem: A client is scheduled for appendectomy. Drag the preoperative nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Preoperative safety starts with identity/consent/site verification, review of readiness data, preparation, medication after checks, and handoff.
steps: Verify the client’s identity, procedure, site, allergies, and consent status.
Review required labs, diagnostic results, medication instructions, NPO status, and baseline vital signs.
Complete ordered preoperative preparation such as skin prep, removal of jewelry, and voiding.
Administer preoperative medications only after required assessments and checklist items are complete.
Communicate handoff information to the perioperative team.

stem: A newly admitted client is recovering from thoracic surgery. Drag the actions to reduce respiratory risk into the correct order.
rationale: Respiratory risk reduction starts with assessment, positioning, pain control, pulmonary hygiene/ambulation, and evaluation.
steps: Assess respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, breath sounds, pain, and sedation level.
Position the client to promote lung expansion unless contraindicated.
Medicate for pain as prescribed to allow coughing and deep breathing.
Assist with incentive spirometry, coughing, deep breathing, and early ambulation as tolerated.
Evaluate breath sounds, oxygenation, and tolerance after interventions.

stem: After turning in bed, An adult client reports a pulling sensation and the abdominal incision opens with visible tissue. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Evisceration requires immediate help, positioning to reduce tension, sterile saline dressings, urgent notification, and monitoring/preparation.
steps: Call for help and stay with the client.
Place the client in a low Fowler or supine position with knees flexed if tolerated.
Cover the protruding tissue with sterile dressings moistened with sterile normal saline.
Notify the surgeon or rapid response team immediately.
Monitor vital signs and prepare the client for emergency treatment.

stem: An adult client is scheduled for a lumbar puncture. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Lumbar puncture care requires preprocedure verification, positioning, support, specimen handling, and postprocedure monitoring.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, anticoagulant use, baseline neurologic status, and required labs.
Have the client void and position in side-lying fetal position or sitting flexed position as directed.
Support the client in maintaining position during the procedure.
Label and send cerebrospinal fluid specimens promptly as ordered.
Monitor neurologic status, puncture site, headache, and vital signs after the procedure.

stem: An older adult client is scheduled for paracentesis. Drag the preprocedure and postprocedure actions into the correct order.
rationale: Paracentesis risk reduction requires baseline assessment, empty bladder, positioning, hemodynamic/site monitoring, and fluid/output documentation.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, vital signs, abdominal girth, and weight.
Ask the client to void immediately before the procedure.
Position the client as directed, usually upright or high Fowler with support.
Monitor blood pressure, puncture site, and symptoms during and after fluid removal.
Record amount and appearance of fluid removed and reassess abdominal girth or weight as ordered.

stem: A client receiving care is scheduled for thoracentesis. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Thoracentesis requires verification, correct positioning, stillness teaching, respiratory monitoring for pneumothorax, and documentation/specimen handling.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, respiratory assessment, and chest imaging availability.
Position the client sitting upright and leaning forward over a bedside table if tolerated.
Instruct the client to remain still and avoid coughing during needle insertion.
Monitor respiratory status, oxygenation, and signs of pneumothorax after the procedure.
Label specimens and document respiratory response, site condition, and fluid characteristics.

stem: An older adult client needs a stool specimen for occult blood. Drag the specimen-collection actions into the correct order.
rationale: Specimen accuracy depends on order/identity/container verification, explanation/hygiene, correct collection, bedside labeling, and timely transport/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, client identity, collection container, timing, and required precautions.
Explain the procedure and perform hand hygiene.
Collect the specimen using correct technique without contamination.
Label the specimen at the bedside with required identifiers.
Send the specimen promptly and document collection.

stem: An older adult client reports epigastric pain with risk factors. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Cardiac symptoms require rapid assessment, oxygen if indicated, prompt ECG, notification of abnormalities, and preparation for ongoing treatment.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain characteristics, vital signs, and level of distress.
Apply oxygen if indicated by assessment and facility protocol.
Obtain a 12-lead ECG as soon as possible according to protocol.
Notify the provider or rapid response team of abnormal findings.
Prepare for prescribed labs, medications, and continuous monitoring.

stem: During labor, the nurse notes variable decelerations on the fetal monitor. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: For nonreassuring fetal patterns, intrauterine resuscitation includes repositioning, assessment, fluids/uterotonic adjustment, oxygen when indicated, and provider notification if unresolved.
steps: Reposition the client to improve uteroplacental blood flow or relieve cord compression.
Assess contraction pattern, maternal blood pressure, and fetal heart rate response.
Increase IV fluid rate if prescribed or per protocol and discontinue uterotonic medication if infusing.
Administer oxygen only if indicated by maternal status or facility protocol.
Notify the provider if the pattern persists or fetal status remains nonreassuring.

stem: The nurse is inserting an indwelling urinary catheter. Drag the procedural steps into the correct order.
rationale: Catheter insertion requires verification/privacy, hygiene/positioning, sterile field, sterile cleansing, insertion with urine return and secure drainage.
steps: Verify the order, assess allergies, explain the procedure, and provide privacy.
Perform hand hygiene and position the client with a waterproof pad.
Open the sterile kit and establish a sterile field.
Cleanse the urinary meatus with sterile technique.
Insert the catheter until urine flows, advance as appropriate, inflate the balloon, secure the catheter, and connect drainage.

stem: A newly admitted client requires peripheral IV access for hydration. Drag the IV insertion actions into the correct order.
rationale: Peripheral IV insertion follows assessment, supply/hygiene preparation, site selection/antisepsis, insertion and connection, then securement/labeling/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, assess allergies, vein condition, limb restrictions, and therapy needs.
Perform hand hygiene, gather supplies, and apply clean gloves.
Apply a tourniquet, select and cleanse the site, and allow antiseptic to dry.
Insert the catheter, confirm blood return, advance the catheter, release the tourniquet, and connect primed tubing or cap.
Secure the catheter, label the dressing, regulate flow, and document site and gauge.

stem: An adult client needs a nasogastric tube for gastric lavage order. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: NG tube care requires assessment, measurement/positioning, insertion, placement confirmation before use, and connection/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, assess nares, gag/swallow ability, contraindications, and explain the procedure.
Measure the tube insertion length and position the client high Fowler if tolerated.
Lubricate and insert the tube while encouraging swallowing as appropriate.
Secure the tube temporarily and obtain the prescribed confirmation of placement before use.
Connect to suction or feeding as prescribed only after placement is verified and document.

stem: A client receiving care is at risk for venous thromboembolism after trauma admission. Drag the SCD actions into the correct order.
rationale: SCD use requires assessment, correct application, functioning device verification, periodic skin checks, and documentation.
steps: Assess skin, pulses, sensation, leg size, contraindications, and provider prescription.
Apply sleeves smoothly to the correct legs and connect tubing.
Turn on the device and verify cyclic inflation.
Remove sleeves periodically to assess skin and provide hygiene according to policy.
Document use, skin checks, and any intolerance or contraindication.

stem: An adult client will receive moderate sedation for colonoscopy. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Moderate sedation requires preprocedure verification, emergency readiness, continuous monitoring during sedation, recovery monitoring, and documentation.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, baseline vital signs, airway assessment, NPO status, and IV access.
Ensure oxygen, suction, reversal agents, and emergency equipment are available.
Administer sedation only as prescribed and monitor level of consciousness, airway, breathing, circulation, and oxygenation.
Continue monitoring during recovery until discharge criteria are met.
Document medications, assessments, interventions, and discharge instructions.

rationale: Critical labs require client assessment, result verification/trending, prompt provider notification, intervention, and documentation.
steps: Assess the client for symptoms and immediate safety concerns related to the result.
Verify the result and compare with previous trends and current treatments.
Notify the provider promptly using a structured communication format.
Implement prescribed interventions and monitoring.
Document the critical result, notifications, read-back, interventions, and client response.

rationale: Critical labs require client assessment, result verification/trending, prompt provider notification, intervention, and documentation.
steps: Assess the client for symptoms and immediate safety concerns related to the result.
Verify the result and compare with previous trends and current treatments.
Notify the provider promptly using a structured communication format.
Implement prescribed interventions and monitoring.
Document the critical result, notifications, read-back, interventions, and client response.

stem: An adult client is scheduled for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Drag the preoperative nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Preoperative safety starts with identity/consent/site verification, review of readiness data, preparation, medication after checks, and handoff.
steps: Verify the client’s identity, procedure, site, allergies, and consent status.
Review required labs, diagnostic results, medication instructions, NPO status, and baseline vital signs.
Complete ordered preoperative preparation such as skin prep, removal of jewelry, and voiding.
Administer preoperative medications only after required assessments and checklist items are complete.
Communicate handoff information to the perioperative team.

stem: An older adult client is recovering from hip replacement. Drag the actions to reduce respiratory risk into the correct order.
rationale: Respiratory risk reduction starts with assessment, positioning, pain control, pulmonary hygiene/ambulation, and evaluation.
steps: Assess respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, breath sounds, pain, and sedation level.
Position the client to promote lung expansion unless contraindicated.
Medicate for pain as prescribed to allow coughing and deep breathing.
Assist with incentive spirometry, coughing, deep breathing, and early ambulation as tolerated.
Evaluate breath sounds, oxygenation, and tolerance after interventions.

stem: After vomiting, A client reports a pulling sensation and the abdominal incision opens with visible tissue. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Evisceration requires immediate help, positioning to reduce tension, sterile saline dressings, urgent notification, and monitoring/preparation.
steps: Call for help and stay with the client.
Place the client in a low Fowler or supine position with knees flexed if tolerated.
Cover the protruding tissue with sterile dressings moistened with sterile normal saline.
Notify the surgeon or rapid response team immediately.
Monitor vital signs and prepare the client for emergency treatment.

stem: A client receiving care is scheduled for a lumbar puncture. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Lumbar puncture care requires preprocedure verification, positioning, support, specimen handling, and postprocedure monitoring.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, anticoagulant use, baseline neurologic status, and required labs.
Have the client void and position in side-lying fetal position or sitting flexed position as directed.
Support the client in maintaining position during the procedure.
Label and send cerebrospinal fluid specimens promptly as ordered.
Monitor neurologic status, puncture site, headache, and vital signs after the procedure.

stem: An adult client is scheduled for paracentesis. Drag the preprocedure and postprocedure actions into the correct order.
rationale: Paracentesis risk reduction requires baseline assessment, empty bladder, positioning, hemodynamic/site monitoring, and fluid/output documentation.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, vital signs, abdominal girth, and weight.
Ask the client to void immediately before the procedure.
Position the client as directed, usually upright or high Fowler with support.
Monitor blood pressure, puncture site, and symptoms during and after fluid removal.
Record amount and appearance of fluid removed and reassess abdominal girth or weight as ordered.

stem: An older adult client is scheduled for thoracentesis. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Thoracentesis requires verification, correct positioning, stillness teaching, respiratory monitoring for pneumothorax, and documentation/specimen handling.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, respiratory assessment, and chest imaging availability.
Position the client sitting upright and leaning forward over a bedside table if tolerated.
Instruct the client to remain still and avoid coughing during needle insertion.
Monitor respiratory status, oxygenation, and signs of pneumothorax after the procedure.
Label specimens and document respiratory response, site condition, and fluid characteristics.

stem: A client needs a sputum culture. Drag the specimen-collection actions into the correct order.
rationale: Specimen accuracy depends on order/identity/container verification, explanation/hygiene, correct collection, bedside labeling, and timely transport/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, client identity, collection container, timing, and required precautions.
Explain the procedure and perform hand hygiene.
Collect the specimen using correct technique without contamination.
Label the specimen at the bedside with required identifiers.
Send the specimen promptly and document collection.

stem: A newly admitted client reports chest pressure. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Cardiac symptoms require rapid assessment, oxygen if indicated, prompt ECG, notification of abnormalities, and preparation for ongoing treatment.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain characteristics, vital signs, and level of distress.
Apply oxygen if indicated by assessment and facility protocol.
Obtain a 12-lead ECG as soon as possible according to protocol.
Notify the provider or rapid response team of abnormal findings.

stem: During labor, the nurse notes prolonged deceleration on the fetal monitor. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: For nonreassuring fetal patterns, intrauterine resuscitation includes repositioning, assessment, fluids/uterotonic adjustment, oxygen when indicated, and provider notification if unresolved.
steps: Reposition the client to improve uteroplacental blood flow or relieve cord compression.
Assess contraction pattern, maternal blood pressure, and fetal heart rate response.
Increase IV fluid rate if prescribed or per protocol and discontinue uterotonic medication if infusing.
Administer oxygen only if indicated by maternal status or facility protocol.
Notify the provider if the pattern persists or fetal status remains nonreassuring.

stem: The nurse is inserting an indwelling urinary catheter. Drag the procedural steps into the correct order. Additional context: night shift in the emergency department; client age 22.
rationale: Catheter insertion requires verification/privacy, hygiene/positioning, sterile field, sterile cleansing, insertion with urine return and secure drainage.
steps: Verify the order, assess allergies, explain the procedure, and provide privacy.
Perform hand hygiene and position the client with a waterproof pad.
Open the sterile kit and establish a sterile field.
Cleanse the urinary meatus with sterile technique.
Insert the catheter until urine flows, advance as appropriate, inflate the balloon, secure the catheter, and connect drainage.

stem: A client receiving care requires peripheral IV access for blood draw and IV start. Drag the IV insertion actions into the correct order.
rationale: Peripheral IV insertion follows assessment, supply/hygiene preparation, site selection/antisepsis, insertion and connection, then securement/labeling/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, assess allergies, vein condition, limb restrictions, and therapy needs.
Perform hand hygiene, gather supplies, and apply clean gloves.
Apply a tourniquet, select and cleanse the site, and allow antiseptic to dry.
Insert the catheter, confirm blood return, advance the catheter, release the tourniquet, and connect primed tubing or cap.
Secure the catheter, label the dressing, regulate flow, and document site and gauge.

stem: A newly admitted client needs a nasogastric tube for persistent vomiting. Drag the actions into the correct order.

Secure the tube temporarily and obtain the prescribed confirmation of placement before use.
Connect to suction or feeding as prescribed only after placement is verified and document.

stem: An older adult client requires peripheral IV access for blood draw and IV start. Drag the IV insertion actions into the correct order.

stem: An older adult client needs a nasogastric tube for persistent vomiting. Drag the actions into the correct order.

Remove sleeves periodically to assess skin and provide hygiene according to policy.

stem: The nurse receives a critical laboratory result: potassium of 6.1 mEq/L. Drag the follow-up actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the medical-surgical unit; client age 27.

stem: An adult client is scheduled for appendectomy. Drag the preoperative nursing actions into the correct order.

Communicate handoff information to the perioperative team.

stem: An adult client is recovering from thoracic surgery. Drag the actions to reduce respiratory risk into the correct order.
rationale: Respiratory risk reduction starts with assessment, positioning, pain control, pulmonary hygiene/ambulation, and evaluation.
steps: Assess respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, breath sounds, pain, and sedation level.
Position the client to promote lung expansion unless contraindicated.
Medicate for pain as prescribed to allow coughing and deep breathing.
Assist with incentive spirometry, coughing, deep breathing, and early ambulation as tolerated.
Evaluate breath sounds, oxygenation, and tolerance after interventions.

stem: After getting out of bed, An adult client reports a pulling sensation and the abdominal incision opens with visible tissue. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Evisceration requires immediate help, positioning to reduce tension, sterile saline dressings, urgent notification, and monitoring/preparation.
steps: Call for help and stay with the client.
Place the client in a low Fowler or supine position with knees flexed if tolerated.
Cover the protruding tissue with sterile dressings moistened with sterile normal saline.
Notify the surgeon or rapid response team immediately.
Monitor vital signs and prepare the client for emergency treatment.

stem: A client receiving care is scheduled for a lumbar puncture. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the medical-surgical unit; client age 19.
rationale: Lumbar puncture care requires preprocedure verification, positioning, support, specimen handling, and postprocedure monitoring.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, anticoagulant use, baseline neurologic status, and required labs.
Have the client void and position in side-lying fetal position or sitting flexed position as directed.
Support the client in maintaining position during the procedure.
Label and send cerebrospinal fluid specimens promptly as ordered.
Monitor neurologic status, puncture site, headache, and vital signs after the procedure.

stem: A newly admitted client is scheduled for paracentesis. Drag the preprocedure and postprocedure actions into the correct order. Additional context: evening shift in the postoperative unit; client age 51.
rationale: Paracentesis risk reduction requires baseline assessment, empty bladder, positioning, hemodynamic/site monitoring, and fluid/output documentation.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, vital signs, abdominal girth, and weight.
Ask the client to void immediately before the procedure.
Position the client as directed, usually upright or high Fowler with support.
Monitor blood pressure, puncture site, and symptoms during and after fluid removal.
Record amount and appearance of fluid removed and reassess abdominal girth or weight as ordered.

stem: A newly admitted client is scheduled for thoracentesis. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order. Additional context: evening shift in the emergency department; client age 51.
rationale: Thoracentesis requires verification, correct positioning, stillness teaching, respiratory monitoring for pneumothorax, and documentation/specimen handling.
steps: Verify consent, allergies, coagulation status, respiratory assessment, and chest imaging availability.
Position the client sitting upright and leaning forward over a bedside table if tolerated.
Instruct the client to remain still and avoid coughing during needle insertion.
Monitor respiratory status, oxygenation, and signs of pneumothorax after the procedure.
Label specimens and document respiratory response, site condition, and fluid characteristics.

stem: A client needs a wound culture. Drag the specimen-collection actions into the correct order.
rationale: Specimen accuracy depends on order/identity/container verification, explanation/hygiene, correct collection, bedside labeling, and timely transport/documentation.
steps: Verify the order, client identity, collection container, timing, and required precautions.
Explain the procedure and perform hand hygiene.
Collect the specimen using correct technique without contamination.
Label the specimen at the bedside with required identifiers.
Send the specimen promptly and document collection.

stem: An older adult client reports shortness of breath with diaphoresis. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Cardiac symptoms require rapid assessment, oxygen if indicated, prompt ECG, notification of abnormalities, and preparation for ongoing treatment.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain characteristics, vital signs, and level of distress.
Apply oxygen if indicated by assessment and facility protocol.
Obtain a 12-lead ECG as soon as possible according to protocol.
Notify the provider or rapid response team of abnormal findings.
Prepare for prescribed labs, medications, and continuous monitoring.

stem: During labor, the nurse notes prolonged deceleration on the fetal monitor. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order. Additional context: day shift in the step-down unit; client age 19.
rationale: For nonreassuring fetal patterns, intrauterine resuscitation includes repositioning, assessment, fluids/uterotonic adjustment, oxygen when indicated, and provider notification if unresolved.
steps: Reposition the client to improve uteroplacental blood flow or relieve cord compression.
Assess contraction pattern, maternal blood pressure, and fetal heart rate response.
Increase IV fluid rate if prescribed or per protocol and discontinue uterotonic medication if infusing.
Administer oxygen only if indicated by maternal status or facility protocol.
Notify the provider if the pattern persists or fetal status remains nonreassuring.

Open the sterile kit and establish a sterile field.
Cleanse the urinary meatus with sterile technique.
Insert the catheter until urine flows, advance as appropriate, inflate the balloon, secure the catheter, and connect drainage.

stem: An older adult client develops wheezing, hypotension, and urticaria after bee sting. Drag the emergency actions into the correct order.
rationale: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. Stop exposure/call help, assess ABCs, give epinephrine, support oxygenation/perfusion, and monitor/escalate.
steps: Stop the suspected exposure and call for emergency assistance.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, level of consciousness, and vital signs.
Administer intramuscular epinephrine as prescribed or per emergency protocol.
Provide high-flow oxygen, position for perfusion, and prepare IV fluids as prescribed.
Monitor response closely and prepare for advanced airway or additional medications.

stem: An older adult client with suspected infected wound has fever, tachycardia, and hypotension. Drag the sepsis actions into the correct order.
rationale: Sepsis response requires rapid assessment, activation/notification, cultures/lactate, timely antibiotics/fluids, and reassessment.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, perfusion, urine output, and vital signs.
Notify the provider or activate sepsis/rapid response protocol.
Obtain ordered cultures and lactate without delaying antibiotics beyond protocol expectations.
Administer prescribed broad-spectrum antibiotics and IV fluids.
Reassess blood pressure, perfusion, urine output, temperature, and laboratory trends.

stem: A client has diabetic ketoacidosis cues including positive ketones. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: DKA care prioritizes assessment and fluid replacement, potassium/rhythm evaluation, insulin infusion, and close metabolic monitoring.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, hydration, and vital signs.
Establish IV access and begin prescribed isotonic fluid replacement.
Check potassium level and cardiac rhythm before or during insulin therapy as prescribed.
Start prescribed regular insulin infusion according to protocol.
Monitor glucose, ketones, electrolytes, intake/output, and neurologic status.

stem: In the medical-surgical unit, a client with diabetes is unconscious and suspected of hypoglycemia. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: For unconscious hypoglycemia, assess ABCs, verify glucose if immediately available, avoid oral intake, give IV dextrose/glucagon per protocol, and reassess.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, and level of consciousness.
Check a point-of-care blood glucose if equipment is immediately available.
Call for help and do not give oral food or fluids.
Administer prescribed IV dextrose or glucagon according to protocol.
Reassess glucose, mental status, and cause of the episode.

stem: A newly admitted client reports crushing chest pain. Drag the nurse’s actions into the correct order.
rationale: ACS care requires assessment, rapid ECG/monitoring, notification/protocol activation, indicated medications after safety checks, and ongoing monitoring.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain, vital signs, and oxygen saturation.
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and place the client on continuous monitoring.
Notify the provider or activate chest-pain protocol.
Administer prescribed medications such as aspirin or nitroglycerin after checking contraindications.
Monitor pain, rhythm, blood pressure, and cardiac biomarkers as prescribed.

stem: An older adult client has sudden unilateral weakness. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Acute stroke care starts with ABCs/glucose/neuro/time assessment, stroke activation, NPO until swallow screen, urgent imaging/labs, and ongoing monitoring.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, glucose, neurologic status, and time last known well.
Activate stroke alert or notify the rapid response/provider according to protocol.
Keep the client NPO until swallowing is screened.
Prepare for urgent brain imaging and laboratory testing.
Monitor neurologic status and vital signs and anticipate reperfusion evaluation if eligible.

stem: A client receiving care develops anxiety and acute hypoxemia after surgery. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Suspected PE requires respiratory/circulatory assessment, oxygen/positioning, urgent notification, preparation for diagnostics/treatment, and monitoring.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, oxygen saturation, lung sounds, and chest pain.
Position the client to ease breathing and apply oxygen as prescribed or per protocol.
Notify the provider or rapid response team immediately.
Prepare for diagnostic testing and prescribed anticoagulation or thrombolytic therapy.
Monitor for worsening hypoxemia, hypotension, dysrhythmias, and bleeding risk.

stem: A client begins having a generalized seizure at the hallway. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Seizure response includes help/timing, injury prevention, airway protection without oral objects, postictal assessment, and documentation.
steps: Call for help and note the time the seizure begins.
Protect the client from injury by lowering to a safe surface and moving hazards away.
Turn the client to the side if possible and maintain airway without placing anything in the mouth.
Assess breathing, oxygenation, and neurologic status after the seizure stops.
Document duration, movements, triggers, postictal state, and provider notification.

stem: An older adult client has a chest tube and the nurse notes: sudden increase in bright-red drainage. Drag the actions into the correct order.
rationale: Chest tube issues require respiratory and system assessment, maintaining drainage mechanics, appropriate immediate intervention, notification for serious findings, and documentation.
steps: Assess respiratory status, oxygen saturation, chest symmetry, and the drainage system.
Ensure the chest tube system remains below chest level and tubing is not kinked or compressed.
Apply the appropriate immediate intervention based on the finding and facility protocol.
Notify the provider promptly for signs of dislodgement, air leak, respiratory distress, or excessive drainage.
Document findings, interventions, drainage amount/character, and client response.

stem: An older adult client is mechanically ventilated and the high-pressure alarm sounds. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Ventilator alarms require assessing the client first, calling help when unstable, checking common causes, correcting/supporting ventilation, and documenting.
steps: Go to the bedside and assess the client first, including oxygenation, chest movement, and distress.
Call for help if the client is unstable or the alarm is not quickly resolved.
Check for common causes such as tubing disconnection, kinks, secretions, biting, or water in tubing.
Suction, reposition, reconnect, or manually ventilate with oxygen as indicated by assessment and policy.
Document the cause, intervention, and client response.

stem: An older adult client has a thermal burn. Drag the early burn-care actions into the correct order.
rationale: Early burn care stops injury, prioritizes ABCDE assessment and hypothermia prevention, removes constrictive items, covers wounds, and prepares fluids/pain control.
steps: Stop the burning process and ensure rescuer safety.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure while preventing hypothermia.
Remove constrictive clothing or jewelry not stuck to the skin.
Cover burns with clean, dry dressings or sheets.
Establish IV access, manage pain, and prepare for fluid resuscitation as prescribed.

stem: A client has potassium 6.4 mEq/L and ECG changes. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes requires assessment, emergency notification, cardiac stabilization, potassium-shifting/removal therapies, and monitoring.
steps: Assess cardiac rhythm, muscle weakness, vital signs, renal status, and IV access.
Notify the provider or activate emergency protocol.
Prepare to administer prescribed cardiac membrane stabilizer such as IV calcium.
Administer prescribed therapies to shift potassium into cells or remove potassium.
Monitor ECG, potassium level, glucose if insulin is used, and response to therapy.

stem: A client receiving care with a head injury shows new vomiting. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Increased ICP requires neurologic/ABC assessment, positioning to promote venous drainage, reduced stimulation, urgent notification, and preparation/monitoring.
steps: Assess airway, breathing, circulation, Glasgow Coma Scale, pupils, motor response, and vital signs.
Elevate the head of bed as prescribed and keep the head midline unless contraindicated.
Minimize stimulation and avoid activities that increase intracranial pressure.
Notify the provider or rapid response team of neurologic deterioration.
Prepare for prescribed interventions and monitor neurologic trends.

stem: After birth, An older adult client has heavy lochia with clots. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage care starts with assessment, fundal massage/help if boggy, IV/uterotonic preparation, quantifying blood loss, and provider/protocol escalation.
steps: Assess fundus, lochia, vital signs, bladder distention, and level of consciousness.
Massage the boggy uterus if present while calling for assistance.
Ensure IV access and prepare to administer prescribed uterotonic medication.
Quantify blood loss and continue frequent assessment.
Notify the provider and prepare for additional hemorrhage protocol interventions.

stem: A pregnant client with preeclampsia begins having a seizure. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: Eclamptic seizure care prioritizes help, injury prevention, airway/oxygenation, magnesium therapy, and maternal-fetal reassessment.
steps: Call for help and stay with the client.
Protect the client from injury and turn the client to the side if possible.
Maintain airway and oxygenation; do not place anything in the mouth.
Prepare to administer magnesium sulfate as prescribed or per protocol.
Assess fetal status, maternal vital signs, reflexes, respiratory status, and urine output after stabilization.

stem: A client receiving peritoneal dialysis has leakage around the catheter. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: PD complications require assessment, stopping/clamping if indicated, notification, sterile specimen collection if ordered, and documentation.
steps: Assess pain, temperature, abdomen, outflow appearance, catheter site, and vital signs.
Stop the exchange or clamp tubing if contamination or severe symptoms occur according to policy.
Notify the provider or dialysis nurse of cloudy drainage, fever, severe pain, or poor return.
Collect a dialysate specimen using sterile technique if prescribed.
Document findings, output amount/character, interventions, and client response.

stem: An adult client with COPD has confusion with dyspnea. Drag the nursing actions into the correct order.
rationale: COPD exacerbation care starts with respiratory assessment, positioning/breathing techniques, prescribed oxygen/bronchodilator therapy, notification if unresolved, and evaluation.
steps: Assess airway, respiratory rate, work of breathing, lung sounds, oxygen saturation, mental status, and sputum.
Position the client upright and encourage pursed-lip breathing.
Administer oxygen at the prescribed target and bronchodilator therapy as ordered.
Notify the provider if distress, hypoxemia, or altered mental status persists.
Evaluate response and monitor for fatigue, carbon dioxide retention cues, and need for escalation.
